title: Self-Tanner on Cotton
timeEstimate: 5–30 minutes IF caught pre-development; hours-plus or escalation if developed
stainChemistry: Self-tanner is dihydroxyacetone (DHA) — an active ingredient that reacts with amino acids in a browning-TYPE REACTION (same browning reaction as cooking) to produce brown browning pigments pigments. On cotton, DHA reacts with residual proteins and fiber groups the same way it does on skin. The brown color develops over 2–8 hours and becomes progressively more resistant as browning pigments lock together. This is effectively a time-bomb stain: invisible when fresh, dark brown hours later, permanent after full development. Cotton tolerates NSD, 3% H₂O₂, acid-side spotters.
whyThisWorks: Speed is everything. Within the first 1–2 hours, cold water + NSD can remove DHA before the browning reaction completes. Once developed, 3% H₂O₂ breaks the browning pigments color; citric / lemon juice acid lightens residual color. After ~8 hours of development, home chemistry plateaus and pro treatment may still leave residue. Expectation-setting is part of the protocol: caught in minutes = trivial; caught next morning = often pro or partial.
defaultAssumption: Self-tanner stains on sheets, towels, or light-colored clothing are usually discovered hours after application (overnight on sheets is the classic). Default is 'developed stain' = hard. Set honest expectations.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: CRITICAL: self-tanner reacts with cotton over 2–8 hours. Speed is everything. If the stain is under 2 hours old, you have a good chance. Over 8 hours, home chemistry may plateau. Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric immediately. Cold only — heat accelerates the browning reaction.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. Lifts DHA and emulsion base before browning reaction completes. Repeat 2–3 cycles for fresh stains.

spottingProtocol.technique: Citric / acetic acid for browning pigments
spottingProtocol.equipment: Lemon juice diluted 1:4 OR dilute acetic acid 5% aqueous
spottingProtocol.instruction: For fully developed brown: dab dilute acid on the shadow. Lightens browning browning pigments. Dwell 5–10 min, rinse cool.
Apply 3% H₂O₂ on residual brown. Oxidizes the browning pigments. Skip on dark cotton. For developed stains, may need repeat cycles with NSD flush between.
COLD wash per care label if stain is still visible — heat sets the browning pigments. Only once the stain is fully gone, hot wash as finisher.

steps: 1. Time fork: <2 hr = good odds, >8 hr = often pro. Cold flush immediately.
2. NSD to strip DHA before reaction completes.
3. Dilute acetic / citric acid on developed browning pigments.
5. Cool wash until stain is fully gone.
products: Dilute acetic acid 5% aqueous OR lemon juice diluted
warnings: Heat accelerates the browning reaction — cold water throughout until stain is gone
Developed stains plateau even with pro chemistry — honest expectations

homeSolutions.instruction: Self-tanner develops over 2–8 hours through a chemical reaction (same browning reaction as cooking). Caught in minutes: trivial. Caught on sheets the next morning: often partial or pro-only. If you just noticed it, drop everything and get cold water on it.
Hold the BACK of the stained fabric under cold running water. Cold only — warm or hot water accelerates the browning. Flush hard for a minute.
Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a fingertip for 1–2 minutes. Rinse cool. Repeat 2–3 times. Dawn strips the DHA before full reaction.
If brown has already developed: mix lemon juice with a little cool water, dab on the spot, let sit 5–10 minutes. The citric acid lightens the browning browning pigments. Rinse cool.
For stubborn brown on white cotton: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide, 5–10 min, blot. Skip on dark cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia.

homeSolutions.agent: COLD Wash Until the Stain Is Gone
homeSolutions.instruction: Wash cold per care label if stain is still visible — hot water sets developed self-tanner into the fabric permanently. Only once the stain is fully clear can you hot-wash as a finisher.

If the self-tanner has been on the fabric 8+ hours (sheets / towels / overnight), home chemistry may plateau. Take to a dry cleaner — and set realistic expectations; even pros sometimes leave faint shadow. Tell them: "Self-tanner on cotton, age [minutes / hours / overnight], home-treated with Dawn + lemon juice + 3% peroxide."

steps: 1. SPEED MATTERS — every hour the browning reaction progresses.
2. Cold water flush from the BACK.
4. Lemon juice / vinegar for developed brown.
6. COLD wash until stain is fully gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if stain is 8+ hours developed and home rounds plateau. Set realistic expectation: even pros may leave shadow.
warnings: HEAT accelerates the browning reaction — cold water until stain is gone
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia
Developed stains may plateau at faint shadow

neverDo: Never delay — browning reaction is a clock
Never hot-wash or dry visible self-tanner — sets the browning pigments
Never promise full removal on developed stains
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates cold chemistry, acid, peroxide
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide
Heat is the enemy — even at wash temp — until stain is fully gone

materialWarnings: browning reaction is time + heat dependent

escalation.when: Self-tanner has been on cotton 8+ hours, has been hot-washed, or two home rounds plateau.
escalation.whatToTell: "Self-tanner on cotton, age [minutes / hours / overnight], home-treated with Dawn + lemon juice + 3% H₂O₂. No hot wash."

contextQuestions: How long ago did the self-tanner touch the fabric?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Self-tanner is the chemistry-clock stain — browning reaction keeps progressing until the DHA is either removed or fully reacted. Speed is half the protocol; honest expectation-setting is the other half.
